title: URL Shortener
description: Design a service that generates short URLs and redirects users to the original long URLs.
statement:
Design a URL shortening service like bit.ly or TinyURL.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How users submit long URLs and receive short URLs
- How the system handles redirection from short to long URLs
- Your choice of database(s) and what they store
- Any APIs or services your system exposes

Focus on the core functionality first. We'll explore scalability, performance, and edge cases in follow-up questions.

title: Image Sharing Service
description: Design a platform where users can upload, store, and share images with metadata like captions and tags.
statement:
Design an image sharing service like Instagram or Imgur.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How users upload images to the service
- Where and how images are stored (blob storage)
- How users view and retrieve images
- Your choice of database(s) for metadata (captions, tags, user info)
- Basic API endpoints your service exposes

Focus on core upload/storage/retrieval functionality. We'll explore image processing, CDNs, and scaling in follow-up questions.

title: Notification Service
description: Design a service that sends notifications to users across multiple channels like email, SMS, and push notifications.
statement:
Design a notification service like Twilio or OneSignal.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How applications request notifications to be sent
- Support for multiple channels (email, SMS, push notifications)
- How you route notifications to the appropriate channel
- Your choice of database(s) and what they store
- How you track delivery status and handle failures

Focus on core notification delivery. We'll explore user preferences, templates, and scaling in follow-up questions.

title: Recommendation System
description: Design a personalized recommendation engine that suggests content to users based on their behavior and preferences.
statement:
Design a recommendation system like Netflix or Spotify.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How user interactions (clicks, views, ratings) are collected and processed
- Your approach to generating recommendations (collaborative filtering, content-based, hybrid)
- Your choice of database(s) for user profiles, item catalogs, and interaction data
- How recommendations are computed and served to users
- Whether you'll use batch or real-time processing

Focus on core recommendation functionality. We'll explore cold start problems, A/B testing, and model retraining in follow-up questions.

title: Search Ranking System
description: Design an ML-powered search system that ranks and returns relevant results based on user queries.
statement:
Design a search ranking system like Google Search or Amazon Product Search.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How search queries are processed and matched against indexed content
- How you'll rank results using ML models (relevance scoring)
- Your choice of database(s) and search indexes
- How you handle personalization and context
- Your approach to model training and feature engineering

Focus on core search and ranking functionality. We'll explore query understanding, learning to rank, and real-time updates in follow-up questions.

title: Fraud Detection System
description: Design a real-time ML system that detects and prevents fraudulent transactions with low latency.
statement:
Design a fraud detection system like Stripe Radar or PayPal's fraud prevention.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How transactions are scored in real-time (milliseconds)
- Your ML model architecture and feature engineering approach
- Your choice of database(s) for storing transaction history and patterns
- How you handle model updates and retraining with new fraud patterns
- Your strategy for balancing false positives vs. false negatives

Focus on core real-time detection functionality. We'll explore rule engines, ensemble models, and feedback loops in follow-up questions.

title: Model Training and Serving Platform
description: Design an MLOps platform for training, deploying, and serving machine learning models at scale.
statement:
Design a model training and serving platform like AWS SageMaker or Google Vertex AI.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How users submit training jobs with datasets and hyperparameters
- Your distributed training infrastructure and resource allocation
- Where and how trained models are stored and versioned
- How models are deployed and served for online/batch predictions
- Your choice of database(s) for experiment tracking and metadata

Focus on core training and serving infrastructure. We'll explore model monitoring, A/B testing deployments, and auto-scaling in follow-up questions.

title: Social Media Feed
description: Design a social media platform that displays personalized feeds of posts from users you follow.
statement:
Design a social media feed system like BlueSky or Threads.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How users create and publish posts
- How users follow other users
- How the system generates a personalized feed for each user
- Your choice of database(s) and what they store
- Any APIs or services your system exposes

Focus on the core feed functionality. We'll explore ranking algorithms, scalability, and real-time updates in follow-up questions.

title: Web Crawler
description: Design a distributed web crawler that discovers, fetches, and indexes web pages across the internet.
statement:
Design a web crawler system like Googlebot or Ahrefs.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How the crawler discovers and queues URLs to visit
- How pages are fetched, parsed, and processed
- Your choice of database(s) and what they store
- How you prevent duplicate crawling of the same URLs
- Any politeness policies (rate limiting, robots.txt)

Focus on the core crawling mechanism. We'll explore distributed crawling, scalability, and content extraction in follow-up questions.

title: Real-Time Analytics System
description: Design a system that collects, processes, and visualizes user behavior analytics from websites and applications in real-time.
statement:
Design a real-time analytics system like Google Analytics or Adobe Analytics.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How events are collected from websites/apps and ingested into the system
- How events are processed and aggregated
- Your choice of database(s) and what they store
- How users query and visualize metrics/reports
- Real-time vs. batch processing components

Focus on core analytics functionality. We'll explore scalability, data accuracy, and advanced features in follow-up questions.

title: Distributed Cache
description: Design a high-performance distributed caching system that stores key-value pairs in memory across multiple nodes.
statement:
Design a distributed cache system like Redis or Memcached.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How clients store and retrieve key-value pairs
- Your cache eviction policy (what happens when memory is full)
- How data is distributed across multiple cache nodes
- Data structures you'll support (strings, lists, sets, etc.)
- Whether and how you'll handle persistence

Focus on the core caching functionality. We'll explore replication, consistency, and failure handling in follow-up questions.

title: Workflow Orchestration System
description: Design a system that schedules and executes complex workflows with task dependencies and state management.
statement:
Design a workflow orchestration system like Airflow or Temporal.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How users define workflows and task dependencies (DAGs)
- How tasks are scheduled and executed by workers
- Your choice of database(s) for workflow state and metadata
- How you handle task failures and retries
- How users monitor workflow execution and view logs

Focus on core orchestration functionality. We'll explore distributed execution, backfilling, and advanced scheduling in follow-up questions.

title: Image Board
description: Design a visual discovery platform with infinite scrolling, collections, and image interactions.
statement:
Design a front-end system for an **image board application** where users can browse, save, and organize visual content.

Your design should support:
- Displaying thousands of images efficiently
- User interactions (saving, organizing into collections)
- Infinite scrolling and search
- Real-time updates when content changes

**Draw a diagram** showing your key UI components, state management approach, data flow, and storage strategy. Label the connections between components and **describe** how your system handles image loading, user actions, and performance optimization.

title: Project Management Tools
description: Design a collaborative workspace with tasks, boards, and real-time updates.
statement:
Design a front-end system for a **project management application** where teams collaborate on tasks and projects.

Your design should support:
- Organizing tasks across multiple views (boards, lists, calendars)
- Drag-and-drop interactions with immediate feedback
- Real-time collaboration with multiple users
- Complex nested data (projects → lists → tasks → subtasks)
- Filtering, searching, and permissions

**Draw a diagram** showing your state management strategy, data synchronization approach, and how UI updates propagate. Label data flows and **describe** how your system handles concurrent edits, optimistic updates, and state consistency.

title: Email client
description: Design a responsive email interface with threading, folders, composition, and sync.
statement:
Design a front-end system for an **email client** where users manage large volumes of messages and conversations.

Your design should support:
- Displaying and navigating thousands of emails efficiently
- Multiple views (inbox, folders, labels, search results)
- Composing emails with drafts and auto-save
- Read/unread states and bulk actions
- Real-time syncing of new messages
- Offline access and conflict resolution

**Draw a diagram** showing your state management architecture, caching strategy, and data synchronization. Label data flows and **describe** how your system handles state updates, optimistic UI changes, and maintaining consistency across views.

title: Chat Application
description: Design a real-time chat application supporting one-on-one and group messaging with message history.
statement:
Design a chat application like WhatsApp or Slack.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How users send and receive messages in real-time
- Support for one-on-one and group conversations
- How messages are stored and retrieved (message history)
- Your choice of database(s) and what they store
- How you handle online/offline status

Focus on core messaging functionality. We'll explore scalability, message delivery guarantees, and features like read receipts in follow-up questions.

title: Video Conferencing System
description: Design a video conferencing platform that enables real-time audio and video communication between multiple participants.
statement:
Design a video conferencing system like Zoom or Microsoft Teams.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How audio and video streams are transmitted between participants
- Your architecture for handling multi-party calls
- How users join/create meetings
- Your choice of database(s) and what they store
- Whether you'll use peer-to-peer or server-based streaming

Focus on core video/audio functionality. We'll explore scalability, quality optimization, and features like screen sharing in follow-up questions.

title: Pub/Sub Messaging System
description: Design a message broker that enables asynchronous communication between publishers and subscribers using topics or queues.
statement:
Design a pub/sub messaging system like Kafka or RabbitMQ.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How publishers send messages to topics/queues
- How subscribers consume messages from topics/queues
- Your message storage and retention strategy
- Your choice of database(s) or storage layer
- How you handle message ordering and delivery guarantees

Focus on core messaging functionality. We'll explore scalability, fault tolerance, and advanced patterns in follow-up questions.

title: Video Streaming Platform
description: Design a platform that allows users to upload, store, and stream video content with adaptive quality.
statement:
Design a video streaming platform like Netflix or YouTube.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How videos are uploaded and processed (encoding/transcoding)
- Where and how video files are stored
- How users stream videos with adaptive bitrate streaming
- Your choice of database(s) for metadata (titles, descriptions, user data)
- How you use CDNs for content delivery

Focus on core video delivery functionality. We'll explore recommendations, live streaming, and global scale in follow-up questions.

title: Live Streaming Platform
description: Design a platform that enables real-time broadcasting of live video content to thousands of concurrent viewers.
statement:
Design a live streaming platform like Twitch or YouTube Live.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How streamers broadcast live video to your platform
- How live streams are ingested, processed, and distributed
- How viewers watch streams with low latency
- Your choice of database(s) for metadata and chat
- How you handle transcoding and CDN distribution

Focus on core live streaming delivery. We'll explore chat systems, DVR/rewind, and scaling to millions of viewers in follow-up questions.

title: Content Delivery Network
description: Design a globally distributed network that caches and delivers content from edge servers close to end users.
statement:
Design a content delivery network (CDN) like Cloudflare or Akamai.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How edge servers (POPs) are distributed globally
- How content is cached at edge locations
- How users are routed to the nearest edge server
- Your cache invalidation/purging strategy
- How origin servers interact with edge servers

Focus on core content caching and delivery. We'll explore cache strategies, anycast routing, and advanced features in follow-up questions.

title: Payment Processing System
description: Design a system that processes online payments securely with support for multiple payment methods and currencies.
statement:
Design a payment processing system like Stripe or PayPal.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How payment requests are initiated and processed
- Payment flow stages (authorization, capture, settlement)
- Your choice of database(s) for transaction records
- How you ensure consistency and handle failures/retries
- Integration with external payment gateways (banks, card networks)

Focus on core payment processing. We'll explore fraud detection, reconciliation, and idempotency in follow-up questions.

title: Stock Trading Platform
description: Design a platform that allows users to buy and sell stocks with real-time pricing and order execution.
statement:
Design a stock trading platform like Robinhood or E*TRADE.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How users place buy and sell orders
- How orders are matched and executed
- Your choice of database(s) for portfolios and transaction history
- How you handle real-time stock price updates
- How you ensure consistency and prevent issues like overselling

Focus on core trading functionality. We'll explore order types, settlement, and high-frequency trading in follow-up questions.

title: Seat Booking System
description: Design a system that allows users to search, select, and book seats for events with concurrency control.
statement:
Design a seat booking system like Ticketmaster or Fandango.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How users search for events and view available seats
- How seat selection and reservation works (preventing double-booking)
- Your choice of database(s) for inventory and bookings
- How you handle concurrent booking requests with locking
- The checkout and payment confirmation flow

Focus on core booking functionality. We'll explore scalability, reservation timeouts, and waitlists in follow-up questions.

title: E-commerce Inventory Management
description: Design a system that tracks product inventory across warehouses and ensures accurate stock levels during orders.
statement:
Design an e-commerce inventory management system like Amazon or Shopify.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe the key components. Include:

- How inventory levels are tracked across multiple warehouses
- How stock is reserved when orders are placed
- Your choice of database(s) for inventory and orders
- How you handle concurrent orders and prevent overselling
- How inventory updates from restocking and returns are processed

Focus on core inventory management. We'll explore warehouse selection strategies, inventory reconciliation, and reserved stock handling in follow-up questions.

title: Collaborative Document Editor
description: Design a real-time collaborative document editing system supporting multiple simultaneous users.
statement:
Design a system that allows multiple users to edit the same document simultaneously with real-time updates.

**Draw and describe** your high-level architecture. Consider:
- How users see each other's changes instantly
- How you'll handle conflicting edits
- What happens when users have poor connectivity
- How documents are stored and retrieved

In follow-up steps, we'll explore conflict resolution strategies, operational transformation, presence awareness, and scaling considerations. Focus on the core data flow for now.

title: Multiplayer Game Server
description: Design a low-latency multiplayer game server supporting thousands of concurrent players.
statement:
Design a system that enables thousands of players to interact in real-time with minimal lag.

**Draw and describe** your high-level architecture. Consider:
- How you'll keep player actions synchronized across clients
- Managing game state updates with low latency
- Handling player disconnections and reconnections
- Supporting matchmaking and player sessions

In follow-up steps, we'll explore client-server authority models, lag compensation techniques, state reconciliation, anti-cheat mechanisms, and horizontal scaling. Start with the core gameplay loop.

title: Live Collaborative Whiteboard
description: Design an infinite canvas whiteboard supporting real-time collaboration with rich visual elements.
statement:
Design a system where multiple users can draw, add shapes, and collaborate on an infinite canvas in real-time.

**Draw and describe** your high-level architecture. Consider:
- How changes to objects are broadcast to all participants
- Rendering updates efficiently across large canvases
- Tracking cursor positions and user presence
- Managing permissions and access control
- Handling offline edits and synchronization

In follow-up steps, we'll explore CRDT vs OT approaches, viewport optimization, vector rendering strategies, and conflict-free merging. Focus on the collaborative update flow.

title: Metrics Monitoring System
description: Design a time-series metrics system for monitoring application and infrastructure performance at scale.
statement:
Design a system that collects, stores, and visualizes millions of metrics per second from distributed applications.

**Draw and describe** your high-level architecture. Consider:
- How metrics are collected from thousands of hosts/services
- Storing time-series data efficiently for fast queries
- Querying and aggregating metrics across dimensions
- Triggering alerts when thresholds are breached
- Handling high ingestion rates and data retention

In follow-up steps, we'll explore time-series databases, cardinality management, downsampling strategies, pull vs push models, and distributed querying. Focus on the ingestion and query path.

title: Distributed Logging System
description: Design a centralized logging platform for collecting, indexing, and searching logs across distributed systems.
statement:
Design a system that ingests billions of log events daily from distributed services and enables fast searching and analysis.

**Draw and describe** your high-level architecture. Consider:
- How logs flow from applications to central storage
- Indexing structured and unstructured log data
- Supporting fast full-text search across massive datasets
- Managing different retention policies per log type
- Handling burst traffic and backpressure

In follow-up steps, we'll explore log parsing pipelines, inverted indexes, sharding strategies, schema evolution, and cost optimization through tiered storage. Focus on ingestion and query paths.

title: Distributed Tracing System
description: Design a distributed tracing platform to track requests across microservices and identify performance bottlenecks.
statement:
Design a system that traces requests as they flow through dozens of microservices, capturing latency and dependencies.

**Draw and describe** your high-level architecture. Consider:
- How trace data is collected from instrumented services
- Linking related operations across service boundaries
- Storing and querying trace spans efficiently
- Sampling strategies to manage data volume
- Visualizing request paths and identifying bottlenecks

In follow-up steps, we'll explore context propagation, tail-based sampling, trace aggregation, anomaly detection, and storage optimization. Focus on the trace collection and correlation flow.

title: Application Performance Monitoring
description: Design an APM system that provides deep visibility into application performance and user experience.
statement:
Design a system that monitors application health, tracks user transactions, and identifies performance issues in production.

**Draw and describe** your high-level architecture. Consider:
- How you'll instrument applications to capture performance data
- Tracking end-to-end transactions across tiers (web, app, database)
- Detecting anomalies and performance degradation automatically
- Correlating application metrics with infrastructure health
- Supporting root cause analysis and code-level diagnostics

In follow-up steps, we'll explore agent instrumentation, baseline detection, dependency mapping, real user monitoring, and metric aggregation strategies. Focus on data collection and analysis paths.

title: Rate Limiter
description: Design a distributed rate limiter that restricts API calls per user across a fleet of API servers.
statement:
Design a distributed rate limiter that restricts API calls per user. Must work across multiple server instances with sub-millisecond decision latency.

**Scale:** 1M users, each limited to 100 requests/minute. 10 API servers sharing state.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- Which rate limiting algorithm you'll use (token bucket, sliding window, fixed window) and why
- How you share rate limit state across 10 distributed API servers
- What happens if your central state store goes down (fail open vs fail closed)
- How clients receive 429 responses with Retry-After headers
- How you handle burst vs sustained traffic differently

We'll explore multi-region rate limiting, Redis clustering, and handling clock skew in follow-up questions.

title: Distributed Key-Value Store
description: Design a distributed key-value store supporting get/put with high availability and tunable consistency.
statement:
Design a distributed key-value store supporting get(key) and put(key, value) with high availability and tunable consistency.

**Scale:** 10M reads/day, 1M writes/day. Values up to 10KB. P99 read latency < 5ms. 3-node minimum replication.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- How you distribute keys across nodes (consistent hashing, virtual nodes)
- Your replication strategy (N replicas, quorum reads/writes: W+R>N)
- How you resolve conflicts when two nodes have different values for the same key
- Your storage engine choice (LSM-tree vs B-tree) and why
- How a new node joins the cluster and acquires data

We'll explore vector clocks, Merkle trees for anti-entropy, and gossip protocol in follow-up questions.

title: Twitter/X Timeline
description: Design the Twitter home timeline — show tweets from followed accounts, sorted by time, with 300M MAU.
statement:
Design the Twitter home timeline — show tweets from accounts a user follows, sorted by time, paginated.

**Scale:** 300M MAU, 150M DAU. 500M tweets/day (5,787/sec). Average user follows 200 accounts. Celebrities have up to 100M followers. Timeline must load in < 200ms p99.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- Fan-out on write vs fan-out on read: trade-offs for each approach
- How you handle celebrities (100M followers) — why naive fan-out breaks
- What a Redis timeline cache looks like (data structure, TTL, what you store)
- How tweets are stored in the primary database
- The API design for paginated timeline fetching

We'll explore the hybrid approach, timeline merging, and media handling in follow-up questions.

title: Notification System
description: Design a notification system supporting email, SMS, and push notifications with guaranteed delivery and priority queues.
statement:
Design a notification system supporting email, SMS, and push notifications with guaranteed delivery and priority queues.

**Scale:** 10M notifications/day. Critical notifications (OTP, fraud alerts) must deliver in < 5 seconds. Marketing notifications can be delayed up to 24 hours. At-least-once delivery guarantee.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- How a notification request flows from the API to delivery across channels
- How you route to the right channel (email/SMS/push) per user preference
- How you prevent sending the same notification twice (idempotency)
- What happens when a push notification provider (FCM, APNS) is temporarily down
- How OTP notifications bypass the queue for immediate delivery

We'll explore circuit breakers, dead letter queues, and per-user rate limiting in follow-up questions.

title: Search Autocomplete / Typeahead
description: Design the search suggestion system that shows autocomplete results as a user types, with < 100ms latency.
statement:
Design the search suggestion system that shows autocomplete results as a user types.

**Scale:** 10M daily active searchers. 10 suggestions returned per prefix. P99 latency < 100ms. Suggestions based on trending searches + personalization. Update suggestions every hour.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- Trie vs inverted index for prefix matching — which scales better and why
- How you rank suggestions (frequency, trending boost, personalization)
- How you cache prefix queries to handle 50M prefix lookups/day
- How often you rebuild the suggestion index and how you handle hot trending searches
- The pipeline from raw search logs to updated trie

We'll explore segmenting by language, Bloom filters, and the aggregation pipeline in follow-up questions.

title: YouTube / Netflix
description: Design a video streaming platform supporting upload, transcoding, and adaptive bitrate streaming to millions of concurrent viewers.
statement:
Design a video streaming platform supporting upload, transcoding, and adaptive bitrate streaming to millions of concurrent viewers.

**Scale:** 500 hours of video uploaded per minute. 1B videos watched per day. Videos range from 100MB (mobile) to 50GB (4K raw). Global audience — latency < 2s to start playback.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- How a 50GB video upload from a mobile phone on a flaky connection works (chunked upload, resumable)
- The transcoding pipeline: who handles 500 hours of video per minute at scale
- CDN strategy: push vs pull, edge node placement
- Adaptive bitrate streaming (HLS/DASH): how the client decides which quality to request
- The metadata storage for videos, user watch history, and view counts

We'll explore shot-based encoding, thumbnail generation, and CDN pre-warming for new releases in follow-up questions.

title: Uber / Swiggy
description: Design a ride-sharing platform that matches riders with nearby drivers in real-time with geospatial queries.
statement:
Design a ride-sharing platform that matches riders with nearby drivers in real-time, handling location updates and surge pricing.

**Scale:** 1M concurrent drivers sending location every 4 seconds. 10M rides/day, peak 200K concurrent rides. Match driver in < 2 seconds from request. Location accuracy: 10 meters.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- How you store and query 1M driver locations that change every 4 seconds (geospatial indexing)
- Geohash vs quadtree: trade-offs for proximity search
- The matching flow: from ride request to driver acceptance including timeouts and re-matching
- How surge pricing is computed and updated every 5 minutes per zone
- Real-time trip tracking: how the rider sees the driver moving on the map

We'll explore geohash precision, cross-region driver pools, and ETA computation in follow-up questions.

title: WhatsApp / Slack
description: Design a real-time messaging system supporting 1:1 and group chats with read receipts and offline delivery.
statement:
Design a real-time messaging system supporting 1:1 chats, group chats, read receipts, and offline message delivery.

**Scale:** 1B daily active users (WhatsApp scale). 100B messages/day (1.16M messages/sec). Group chats up to 1024 members. At-least-once delivery. Offline users: store messages for up to 30 days.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- WebSocket connections: with 1B users, how many connection servers do you need and how are users assigned?
- How a message from User A reaches User B who is connected to a different WebSocket server
- Group message fan-out: 1 message → up to 1024 copies — how do you handle this efficiently?
- Offline delivery: how do you store messages for offline users and deliver them when they reconnect?
- End-to-end encryption: what does this mean for your server-side architecture?

We'll explore consistent hashing for connection servers, Redis pub/sub routing, and Cassandra for message storage in follow-up questions.

title: Google Drive
description: Design a cloud file storage system with sync across devices, sharing, versioning, and deduplication.
statement:
Design a cloud file storage system with sync, sharing, versioning, and collaborative editing across devices.

**Scale:** 1B registered users, 100M DAU. 10M file uploads/day. Average file size: 1MB (mostly documents, some videos). Files up to 5TB supported. Sync across unlimited devices per user. File history: retain last 100 versions.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- How you efficiently sync a 100MB file that had only 1KB changed (chunking and delta sync)
- Content-based deduplication: if 1000 users upload the same file, how do you store it once?
- The chunk/block approach to file storage (chunk size, hashing, storage layer)
- How you handle conflict when two devices edit the same file simultaneously
- The API design for resumable chunked uploads

We'll explore Lamport timestamps, CRDTs for collaborative text editing, and tier storage in follow-up questions.

title: Payment System
description: Design a payment processing system handling transactions with zero-tolerance for double charges and financial consistency.
statement:
Design a payment processing system handling transactions, preventing double charges, and ensuring financial consistency.

**Scale:** 1M transactions/day, 100K peak TPS during sales events. Zero tolerance for double charges or money loss. Multi-currency support. Integration with 20+ payment providers. PCI DSS compliance required.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- How you prevent the same payment from being charged twice (client retry on timeout scenario)
- What happens if your service crashes between 'charge the card' and 'update the order' (distributed transaction problem)
- Idempotency key: what it is, how Stripe uses it, how you implement it
- Saga pattern vs two-phase commit for distributed transactions across payment + inventory + order services
- The outbox pattern for reliably publishing payment events to downstream services

We'll explore reconciliation jobs, audit logs, and multi-currency exchange rates in follow-up questions.

title: Distributed ID Generator
description: Design a system that generates globally unique IDs at high throughput, roughly time-sortable, without coordination.
statement:
Design a system that generates globally unique IDs at high throughput, roughly time-sortable, without coordination between nodes.

**Scale:** 10K IDs/sec per node, 100 nodes = 1M IDs/sec total. IDs must be globally unique, roughly time-sortable, no single point of failure, fit in 64 bits (BIGINT).

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- Why UUID v4 (random) might be bad for database index performance
- How Twitter Snowflake works: 41-bit timestamp + 10-bit machine ID + 12-bit sequence counter
- How machine IDs are assigned at startup (ZooKeeper/etcd coordination)
- What happens if two machines have different system clocks (clock skew handling)
- UUIDv7 vs Snowflake: similarities, differences, when to use each

We'll explore clock rollback handling, batch ID generation, and multi-datacenter ID namespacing in follow-up questions.

title: Recommendation Engine
description: Design a product recommendation system for an e-commerce platform combining collaborative filtering with real-time signals.
statement:
Design a product recommendation system for an e-commerce platform (Flipkart/Amazon scale) combining collaborative filtering with real-time behavioral signals.

**Scale:** 100M users, 10M products. Recommendations updated every 30 minutes. Real-time signal: last 3 actions within session boost relevance. Cold start: new users get editorial recommendations. P99 recommendation latency < 50ms.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- Collaborative filtering: user-based vs item-based — which scales better to 100M users and why
- How to compute recommendations without a 100M × 10M matrix factorization on every request
- What a feature store is and why you need one for real-time ML inference
- Two-stage ranking: offline batch (collaborative filtering) + online re-ranking (lightweight model)
- How A/B testing works when you're running multiple recommendation algorithms simultaneously

We'll explore cold start solutions, session-based recommendations, and model monitoring in follow-up questions.

title: Live Streaming Platform
description: Design a live streaming platform with 25M concurrent viewers, low latency, and real-time chat.
statement:
Design a platform for live video streaming with millions of concurrent viewers, low latency, and chat.

**Scale:** 25M concurrent viewers peak (Hotstar IPL scale). Stream latency from broadcaster to viewer < 10 seconds (LL-HLS). Chat: 10K messages/minute during peak. Global audience.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- Why you can't use the same CDN strategy for live vs on-demand video
- The RTMP ingest pipeline: from broadcaster's OBS to viewer's browser
- How 25M concurrent viewers at 4 Mbps each maps to CDN capacity requirements
- Chat fan-out at IPL scale: how do you deliver 10K messages/minute to 25M viewers?
- Low-latency HLS (LL-HLS): how 2-second segments differ from standard 10-second segments

We'll explore DVR/rewind, stream health monitoring, and multi-region chat partitioning in follow-up questions.

title: Analytics Platform
description: Design an analytics platform that ingests clickstream events, processes them in real-time, and serves sub-second dashboard queries.
statement:
Design an analytics platform that ingests clickstream events from 100M users, processes them in real-time, and serves dashboards.

**Scale:** 100M users × 10 events/session × 5 sessions/day = 5B events/day (57.8K events/sec). Real-time metrics update within 5 seconds. Historical queries over 2 years. Dashboard query latency < 1 second for last-day metrics.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- Lambda architecture: batch layer + speed layer — where each shines
- Kappa architecture: streaming only — when this makes more sense
- How many Kafka partitions you need for 57.8K events/sec and how you partition
- Pre-aggregation strategy: why compute hourly rollups rather than querying 5B raw events
- Funnel analysis: how to track a user journey across up to 10 steps efficiently

We'll explore ClickHouse for OLAP, Flink for stream processing, and tiered data retention in follow-up questions.

title: Mint.com
description: Design a personal finance aggregation service connecting to banks, categorizing transactions, and tracking budgets.
statement:
Design a personal finance aggregation service that connects to banks, categorizes transactions, and tracks budgets.

**Scale:** 10M users, each with 2–3 connected bank accounts. Poll bank APIs every hour for new transactions. Categorize transactions with < 5% error rate. Budget alerts within 5 minutes of threshold breach.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- How you securely connect to banks (Plaid OAuth flow, token storage)
- Transaction categorization: rule-based vs ML model — maintenance trade-offs
- Polling 10M × 2.5 accounts hourly = 25M bank API calls/hour — how do you manage rate limits?
- Where ACID transactions matter in this system vs where eventual consistency is acceptable
- Budget alert delivery: how do you detect threshold breaches and notify in under 5 minutes?

We'll explore credential encryption, ML model retraining, and bank API failure handling in follow-up questions.

title: Real-Time Leaderboard
description: Design Amazon/Flipkart's bestseller ranking feature — near-real-time product rankings by category with Big Billion Day scale.
statement:
Design Amazon/Flipkart's bestseller ranking feature — compute and display product rankings by category updated in near-real-time.

**Scale:** 10M products across 1000 categories. Rankings updated every minute. Top 100 per category surfaced to users. During Big Billion Day: 50K orders/sec.

**Your task:** Create a high-level system design diagram and describe:
- How Redis sorted sets (ZINCRBY) can power real-time rankings and the throughput they support
- Whether you need per-minute, per-hour, or per-day accuracy — and how the answer changes the architecture
- How you combine historical sales rank + recent sales velocity into a single score
- At 1000 categories × 10M products, what is the Redis memory footprint and how do you manage it?
- How rankings are exposed via API with appropriate caching

We'll explore sliding window velocity computation, Kafka order events, and weekly Spark batch jobs in follow-up questions.

title: Parking Lot
description: Design an OOP model for a multi-floor parking lot that handles different vehicle types and ticketing.
statement:
Design a parking lot system.

Support multiple floors, different vehicle types (bike, car, truck), ticket generation on entry, and fee calculation on exit.

Focus on class design, relationships, and the core booking/release flow.

title: Elevator System
description: Design the class structure and scheduling algorithm for a multi-elevator building system.
statement:
Design an elevator control system for a building with multiple elevators.

Handle floor requests, elevator scheduling (nearest car algorithm), door state, and direction.

Focus on OOP design, state machines, and the dispatch algorithm.

title: Chess Game
description: Design the object model for a two-player chess game including move validation and game state.
statement:
Design a chess game.

Model the board, pieces, move validation, check/checkmate detection, and game state.

Focus on class hierarchy for pieces, the board representation, and how moves are validated.

title: LRU Cache
description: Design and implement a thread-safe LRU cache with O(1) get and put operations.
statement:
Design a thread-safe LRU (Least Recently Used) cache.

Support O(1) get and put. Handle concurrent access safely. Discuss eviction policy and capacity management.

Focus on the data structure choice, concurrency strategy, and API design.

title: Vending Machine
description: Design a vending machine that handles product selection, payment processing, and change dispensing.
statement:
Design a vending machine.

Handle product inventory, multiple payment methods (coin, card), change dispensing, and refund handling.

Focus on state machine design, payment abstraction, and error handling.

title: Hotel Booking System
description: Design the class structure for a hotel booking system with room management and reservation handling.
statement:
Design a hotel booking system.

Handle room types, availability, reservations, check-in/check-out, and cancellation policies.

Focus on the entity model, concurrency for double-booking prevention, and the booking lifecycle.

title: Library Management System
description: Design an OOP model for a library system with book catalog, member management, and lending.
statement:
Design a library management system.

Handle book catalog, member accounts, book lending/returning, overdue fines, and search.

Focus on entity relationships, the lending lifecycle, and fine calculation logic.

title: Food Delivery App
description: Design the class model for a food delivery platform handling restaurants, menus, orders, and delivery tracking.
statement:
Design a food delivery application.

Handle restaurant onboarding, menu management, order placement, delivery assignment, real-time tracking, and payment.

Focus on the order state machine, delivery partner assignment, and the restaurant-menu-item hierarchy.

title: ATM Machine
description: Design the software model for an ATM supporting withdrawals, deposits, balance inquiries, and PIN management.
statement:
Design an ATM machine.

Handle card insertion, PIN validation, withdrawal (with denomination selection), deposit, balance inquiry, and session timeout.

Focus on state machine design, cash dispensing algorithm, and secure session handling.

title: Social Network (Friends/Feed)
description: Design the class model for core social network features: user profiles, friend connections, and post feeds.
statement:
Design the low-level model for a social network.

Handle user profiles, friend requests/connections, post creation, feed generation (simple, non-ML), and notifications.

Focus on the graph model for connections, feed aggregation approach, and notification dispatch.

title: Handling Team Conflict
description: Describe a time you navigated a significant disagreement within your team.
statement:
Tell me about a time you had a significant conflict with a teammate or manager.

Walk me through: the situation, your role, the specific disagreement, what you did to resolve it, and the outcome.

Use the STAR format. I will follow up on your decision-making and leadership.

title: Delivery Under Pressure
description: Describe a time you delivered a project under extreme time pressure or with significant technical challenges.
statement:
Tell me about a time you had to deliver something important under significant time pressure or constraints.

Walk me through: the situation, what made it hard, the trade-offs you made, and what you shipped.

Use the STAR format. I will probe on what you cut, what you kept, and how you communicated.

title: Disagreeing with Technical Direction
description: Describe a time you disagreed with a technical decision made by your team or leadership.
statement:
Tell me about a time you disagreed with a technical decision — whether from your team, a peer, or leadership.

Walk me through: the decision, why you disagreed, how you raised it, and what happened.

I will probe on how you handled it when you were overruled, and how you maintained a working relationship.

title: Navigating Ambiguity
description: Describe a time you had to make significant progress on a problem that was poorly defined.
statement:
Tell me about a time you had to make significant progress on a problem that was poorly defined or where requirements were unclear.

Walk me through: the situation, how you clarified scope, what decisions you made independently, and the outcome.

I will probe on how you balanced moving fast vs. getting clarity.

title: Mentoring a Junior Engineer
description: Describe a time you helped grow a junior engineer on your team.
statement:
Tell me about a time you mentored or coached a junior engineer.

Walk me through: the engineer, the challenge they were facing, what you did to help, and the outcome.

I will probe on your coaching approach and how you measured their growth.

title: Cross-Functional Collaboration
description: Describe a time you drove alignment across multiple teams or functions to ship something.
statement:
Tell me about a time you had to align and coordinate across multiple teams or functions to ship a project.

Walk me through: the project, who was involved, what made alignment hard, and how you drove it.

I will probe on how you handled teams with competing priorities.

title: A Significant Failure
description: Describe a significant professional failure and what you learned from it.
statement:
Tell me about a significant professional failure — something where the outcome was bad and you were responsible.

Walk me through: what happened, your role in it, the impact, and what you learned and changed.

I will probe on ownership, self-awareness, and the concrete changes you made.

title: Influence Without Authority
description: Describe a time you drove a significant change without having direct authority over the people involved.
statement:
Tell me about a time you drove a significant change or decision without having formal authority over the people involved.

Walk me through: the change, who needed to be influenced, your approach, and the outcome.

I will probe on how you built buy-in and what you did when people pushed back.

title: Data-Driven Decision
description: Describe a time you used data to make or change a significant technical or product decision.
statement:
Tell me about a time you used data to make or change a significant decision.

Walk me through: the decision, what data you gathered, how you analyzed it, and what changed as a result.

I will probe on what data you chose, what you ignored, and how confident you were in the conclusion.

title: Scaling a System or Team
description: Describe a time you had to scale something — a system, a team, or a process — that was not ready to scale.
statement:
Tell me about a time you had to scale something — a technical system, a team, or a process — that was not designed for the load it needed to handle.

Walk me through: the situation, what the breaking points were, what you did, and the outcome.

I will probe on the trade-offs you made and what you would do differently.

title: Test a File Upload API
description: Design a test strategy for an API endpoint that accepts file uploads (images, documents) and stores them.
statement:
You're asked to test a REST endpoint `POST /upload` that accepts a file (images and PDFs), validates it, and stores it in object storage, returning a URL.

**Your task:** Walk through how you'd test this end to end — scope and assumptions, the test cases you'd write (positive, negative, boundary), how you'd automate it, and what non-functional and quality concerns matter. Be concrete: give actual inputs and expected results, not just categories.

title: Test a Login / Authentication Flow
description: Design test coverage for a username/password login with session handling and lockout.
statement:
A web app has a login flow: username + password, session cookie on success, account lockout after repeated failures, and a 'forgot password' path.

**Your task:** Lay out how you'd test it — functional, negative, boundary, and security cases — plus your automation approach and which non-functional aspects (performance, security) you'd cover. Give concrete inputs and expected outcomes.

title: Test a Vending Machine
description: The classic: design test cases for a vending machine that takes coins and dispenses items.
statement:
A vending machine accepts coins, lets a user select an item, dispenses it, and returns change. It tracks inventory.

**Your task:** Enumerate how you'd test it — happy paths, edge cases (exact change, insufficient funds, out of stock, simultaneous selection), and state transitions. Then describe how you'd automate this and what would be hardest to test reliably.

title: Test a Ride-Sharing Surge Pricing Service
description: Design a test strategy for a service that computes dynamic surge multipliers from demand and supply.
statement:
A ride-sharing service computes a surge multiplier per region from real-time demand (riders) and supply (drivers), updating every few seconds and feeding the fare calculator.

**Your task:** Design the test strategy — how you'd scope it, the levels of testing you'd apply, concrete cases (including boundary multipliers, stale data, region edge cases, concurrency), how you'd automate it without a live fleet, and the non-functional and quality-gate concerns. This one is hard on purpose; show risk-based prioritization.

title: Test a Payment Checkout
description: Design test coverage for an e-commerce checkout that charges a card and creates an order.
statement:
An e-commerce checkout takes a cart, applies discounts and tax, charges a payment gateway, and creates an order. Payment can succeed, fail, or time out; orders must never be double-charged.

**Your task:** Walk through testing this — functional and negative cases, boundary and idempotency concerns, how you'd handle the external payment gateway in tests (mocking/sandbox), and the non-functional, security, and quality-gate aspects. Be concrete about inputs and expected results.
